title: IELTS Speaking Assessment — LLM Transcript Judgment (FC/LR/GRA/P)

config.operatorRule: NEVER land hardcoded / AI-guessed score defaults to fill empty CallerTarget rows. Honest empty bands surface gaps; fake scores corrupt EMA. (Epic #2135.)

story.iWant: To score every learner speaking transcript against the four official IELTS Speaking criteria (Fluency & Coherence, Lexical Resource, Grammatical Range & Accuracy, Pronunciation) using LLM transcript judgment
story.soThat: Mastery progression, adaptive feedback, and completion gating work for every IELTS caller from session 1 — without requiring a prosody vendor; when a vendor is later wired, it ENHANCES FC + P only via the prosody-raw consumer (S3).

context.applies: IELTS Speaking-profile courses only — gated by Subject.teachingProfile = 'ielts-speaking' via lib/pipeline/specs-loader.ts::filterByTeachingProfile.
assumptions: Transcript contains learner speech (Part 1 interview, Part 2 long turn, or Part 3 discussion).

OPERATOR RULE — NEVER fabricate scores. If a criterion has insufficient learner evidence in the transcript, the corresponding score MUST be null and hasLearnerEvidence MUST be false. Honest empty bands surface gaps; fake scores corrupt the EMA aggregator.

Band anchors reference HF's active IELTS slot mapping: Band 3 (approachingEmerging) → Band 4 (emerging) → Band 5.5 (developing) → Band 7 (secure), per lib/banding/presets.ts::TIER_PRESETS['ielts-speaking'].

Pronunciation (P) uses DUAL-CONFIDENCE SCORING — scored from transcript cues (self-corrections, hesitations, repair patterns, length variance, long-turn cleanliness, mis-pronunciation feedback) whenever there is any speech, with a `confidence` field capped at 'medium' from transcript alone. The S3 prosody-augmentation writer (different parameter, post-S3) raises confidence to 'high' when vendor signal arrives. LR + GRA + FC remain LLM-only forever and are definitionally transcript-judged (no confidence tier — the call is the evidence).
Per-criterion scores are normalised to 0–1 by dividing the IELTS band (1–9) by 9 (e.g. Band 7 → 0.778). The downstream SKILL-AGG-001 EMA pipeline expects 0–1 inputs.

frameworks.ielts: IELTS Speaking Public Band Descriptors — Bands 1-9, four criteria (FC, LR, GRA, P). British Council / IDP / Cambridge Assessment English.

acceptanceCriteria.title: Fluency & Coherence scored
acceptanceCriteria.given: A learner speaking transcript with sufficient turn-taking and connected speech
acceptanceCriteria.when: The system analyses fluency markers (pace, hesitation, self-correction, connectives) and coherence markers (topic-tracking, logical progression, signposting)
acceptanceCriteria.then: A fluency_and_coherence score 0–1 (representing IELTS band / 9) is produced, OR null if insufficient evidence
acceptanceCriteria.rationale: FC is the surface-most criterion — can the learner keep speaking at length without breakdown, and does what they say hang together? (IELTS Public Descriptors §FC)

acceptanceCriteria.title: Lexical Resource scored
acceptanceCriteria.given: A learner transcript containing topical, idiomatic, paraphrase, and collocation choices
acceptanceCriteria.when: The system analyses range (varied vocabulary), precision (apt word choice), flexibility (paraphrase when stuck), and idiomaticity (less common items used naturally)
acceptanceCriteria.then: A lexical_resource score 0–1 (representing IELTS band / 9) is produced, OR null if insufficient evidence
acceptanceCriteria.rationale: LR is text-content-only — vendor audio cannot see vocabulary. LLM transcript judgment is the only valid signal. (IELTS Public Descriptors §LR)

acceptanceCriteria.title: Grammatical Range & Accuracy scored
acceptanceCriteria.given: A learner transcript containing sentence structures (simple, compound, complex) and a mix of correct and erroneous forms
acceptanceCriteria.when: The system analyses range (variety of structures), complexity (subordination, conditionals, modals), and accuracy (error frequency, error gravity, error type)
acceptanceCriteria.then: A grammatical_range_and_accuracy score 0–1 (representing IELTS band / 9) is produced, OR null if insufficient evidence
acceptanceCriteria.rationale: GRA is text-content-only — vendor audio cannot judge grammar. LLM transcript judgment is the only valid signal. (IELTS Public Descriptors §GRA)

acceptanceCriteria.title: Pronunciation scored from transcript cues with honest confidence (vendor-augmentable)
acceptanceCriteria.given: A learner transcript with any speech evidence (any combination of self-corrections, tutor pronunciation feedback, hesitations, fillers, repair patterns, length variance, or long-turn cleanliness)
acceptanceCriteria.when: The system analyses transcript-cued pronunciation evidence — explicit self-corrections, tutor feedback like 'try saying X', misheard items the tutor needed to confirm, repair patterns, and sustained-turn cleanliness
acceptanceCriteria.then: A pronunciation score 0–1 (representing IELTS band / 9) is produced WHENEVER there is any speech evidence, accompanied by a confidence tier ('low' | 'medium' | 'high'). Transcript-only evidence MUST cap confidence at 'medium'; 'high' is reserved for the S3 prosody-augmentation pathway. Null is returned ONLY when there is no learner speech at all.
acceptanceCriteria.rationale: Without audio, pronunciation evidence is sparser than the other criteria — but never zero when there is speech. Null-when-vendor-absent would silently zero every P slot before S3 ships. Dual-confidence scoring is honest: P is always scored when there is speech, and the confidence tier carries the transcript-mode limitation forward to consumers. Per epic #2135 S3, prosody-raw augments this slot and raises confidence to 'high'.

constraints.description: Pronunciation MUST use dual-confidence scoring. Score P from transcript cues whenever there is any speech evidence; emit a `confidence` tier of 'low' | 'medium' | 'high'. Transcript-only evidence MUST cap confidence at 'medium' — 'high' is reserved for the S3 prosody-augmentation pathway (epic #2135 S3). Return null + hasLearnerEvidence: false ONLY when there is no learner speech at all. Do NOT infer phonological accuracy from text-only signals (transcript spelling reflects the ASR's interpretation, not the learner's actual pronunciation); the confidence tier carries the transcript-mode limitation forward.

Scores MUST be normalised to 0–1 by dividing the IELTS band (1–9) by 9 before returning. The downstream SKILL-AGG-001 EMA expects 0–1 inputs. Band 3 → 0.333, Band 4 → 0.444, Band 5.5 → 0.611, Band 7 → 0.778.

failureConditions.implication: Transcript contains fewer than 40 words of learner speech across the call. Set ALL four scores to null and hasLearnerEvidence to false. Do NOT score on insufficient evidence. The honest empty band is what the operator rule preserves.

parameters.name: fluency_and_coherence
parameters.description: IELTS Fluency & Coherence — sustained speaking without breakdown, connected discourse with logical organisation.
parameters.formula: ielts_band(fc) / 9 — normalised against the IELTS Public Band Descriptors for Fluency & Coherence.

subMetrics.name: Fluency
subMetrics.description: Ability to keep going — speech rate, hesitation pattern, self-correction without breakdown

definitions.high: Speaks at length without noticeable effort; hesitations are content-related, not language-related; self-corrections are minor
definitions.low: Long pauses to search for words; frequent false-starts; speech breaks down before complete idea is delivered

subMetrics.name: Coherence
subMetrics.description: Organisation of ideas — topic development, logical progression, signposting and connectives

definitions.high: Develops topics fully and appropriately; uses a range of cohesive devices flexibly; ideas link clearly
definitions.low: Topics are barely developed; few or repetitive connectives; relationships between ideas are unclear

interpretationScale.label: Band 3 — Limited User
interpretationScale.implication: Long pauses, frequent breakdowns, basic linking only ('and', 'but')

interpretationScale.label: Band 4 — Limited User
interpretationScale.implication: Cannot respond without noticeable pauses; speech may be slow with frequent repetition; links basic sentences but with repetitious use of simple connectives

interpretationScale.label: Band 5.5 — Modest User
interpretationScale.implication: Usually maintains flow of speech but uses repetition, self-correction and/or slow speech to keep going; over-uses certain connectives and discourse markers

interpretationScale.label: Band 7 — Good User
interpretationScale.implication: Speaks at length without noticeable effort or loss of coherence; may demonstrate language-related hesitation at times; uses a range of connectives and discourse markers with some flexibility

interpretationScale.label: Band 8+ — Very Good / Expert User
interpretationScale.implication: Speaks fluently with only occasional repetition or self-correction; develops topics coherently and appropriately

scoringAnchors.example: Tutor: 'Tell me about your hometown.' Learner: '...uh... my home... is... Spain. Madrid. Is... uh... big.'
scoringAnchors.rationale: Band 3 — extreme breakdown, words rather than phrases, no topic development.

scoringAnchors.example: 'I live in Madrid. It is a big city. There are many people. The food is good. I like the food. And the weather is hot in summer.'
scoringAnchors.rationale: Band 4 — simple sentences strung together with basic connectives, minimal topic development, no real coherence devices.

scoringAnchors.example: 'I'm from Madrid, which is the capital of Spain. It's quite a busy city, you know, with lots of people from different — um, different backgrounds. The thing I like most is probably the food. And, well, the weather can be hot but it's nice.'
scoringAnchors.rationale: Band 5.5 — maintains flow with some hedging and self-correction; uses 'which' subordination and discourse markers ('you know', 'well') though repetitively.

scoringAnchors.example: 'I'm originally from Madrid — which, despite being the capital, has a really distinctive neighbourhood feel. What I appreciate most is the way different barrios each have their own character. So you can move ten minutes and feel like you've crossed into another world. That's something I think Madrid does better than other European capitals.'
scoringAnchors.rationale: Band 7 — sustained speech, varied connectives ('which', 'despite', 'so', 'that'), explicit topic development with a comparative judgment, language-related hesitation absent.

promptGuidance.whenHigh: Strong FC. Push complexity: longer Part 3 abstract discussion, faster topic switches, comparative judgments across cultures or time.
promptGuidance.whenLow: FC under strain. Use shorter Part 1 questions, slow the pace, model connectives explicitly ('first... then... finally...'), prioritise breakdown-recovery over polish.
promptGuidance.whenMedium: Mid-band FC. Push for sustained Part 2 long turns (≥90s), introduce one new discourse marker per session, gently flag repetitive connectives.

parameters.promptTemplate:
RUBRIC[skill_fluency_and_coherence_fc] — IELTS Fluency & Coherence (transcript-derivable).
Score the LEARNER on Fluency & Coherence using IELTS Public Band Descriptors. Output a number 0-1 (IELTS band / 9) OR null if insufficient evidence.

IELTS BAND ANCHORS (HF-active slots):
- Band 3 (0.333): Long pauses, frequent breakdowns; only basic connectives ('and', 'but'); responses often single words or short phrases.
- Band 4 (0.444): Cannot respond without noticeable pauses; slow speech with frequent repetition; basic sentences linked with repetitious simple connectives.
- Band 5.5 (0.611): Usually maintains flow but uses repetition, self-correction or slow speech to keep going; over-uses certain connectives/discourse markers; some topic development.
- Band 7 (0.778): Speaks at length without noticeable effort; language-related hesitation rare; uses a range of connectives and discourse markers with some flexibility; coherent topic development.

WHAT TO LOOK FOR:
- Fluency signals: speech rate, hesitation pattern (filler 'um/uh' frequency vs content-related pauses), self-correction frequency, false-starts, breakdown points.
- Coherence signals: range and accuracy of connectives, signposting ('first... however... in conclusion...'), topic-tracking across multiple turns, logical progression within a long turn.

NULL HANDLING (operator rule — never fabricate):
If the transcript contains fewer than ~40 words of learner speech, OR the learner produced only one-word answers throughout, set this score to NULL and set hasLearnerEvidence to false. Do NOT guess a 'balanced' 0.5 default. The empty band is the honest output and the EMA aggregator handles nulls by skipping.

EVIDENCE: Cite one to three short transcript quotes (≤15 words each) that justify your band judgment.

parameters.name: lexical_resource
parameters.description: IELTS Lexical Resource — vocabulary range, precision of word choice, paraphrase flexibility, idiomatic appropriacy. TRANSCRIPT-ONLY — vendor audio cannot judge.
parameters.formula: ielts_band(lr) / 9 — normalised against the IELTS Public Band Descriptors for Lexical Resource.

subMetrics.name: Range
subMetrics.description: Breadth of vocabulary — topical, idiomatic, less common items

definitions.high: Uses a wide range of vocabulary including less common and idiomatic items, with some inaccuracy at the margins
definitions.low: Uses simple vocabulary, often repetitive; cannot discuss most topics; relies on memorised phrases

subMetrics.name: Precision
subMetrics.description: Appropriacy of word choice to meaning and context

definitions.high: Word choice is apt and natural; collocations are conventional; meaning conveyed precisely
definitions.low: Frequent wrong word choice; unnatural collocations; meaning often distorted or unclear

subMetrics.name: Flexibility (paraphrase)
subMetrics.description: Ability to paraphrase when an exact word is not available

definitions.high: Paraphrases successfully when stuck, often with effective circumlocution
definitions.low: Stops at unknown words; cannot find another way to express the idea

interpretationScale.label: Band 3 — Limited User
interpretationScale.implication: Very basic vocabulary; cannot discuss most topics; meaning often opaque

interpretationScale.label: Band 4 — Limited User
interpretationScale.implication: Sufficient vocabulary for familiar topics only; rarely paraphrases; frequent inappropriate word choice

interpretationScale.label: Band 5.5 — Modest User
interpretationScale.implication: Successful paraphrase emerging across familiar and unfamiliar topics; vocabulary remains mostly literal. Limited flexibility; some less-common items may be attempted but they are not required to score at this band.

interpretationScale.label: Band 7 — Good User
interpretationScale.implication: Uses vocabulary resource flexibly to discuss a variety of topics; uses some less common and idiomatic items with occasional inaccuracy; paraphrases effectively. Idiom and less-common vocabulary first become required at Band 7 — they are the lift from the previous band.

interpretationScale.label: Band 8+ — Very Good / Expert User
interpretationScale.implication: Wide range of vocabulary used fluently and precisely; skilful use of less common and idiomatic vocabulary

scoringAnchors.example: 'I like... thing. Music. Music is good. I like music. Music make me happy. I like music every day.'
scoringAnchors.rationale: Band 3 — extreme repetition of 'music' and 'like', single content word per clause, no paraphrase attempted.

scoringAnchors.example: 'I like music very much. Pop music. Sometimes I listen to rock. The songs make me happy. I have many songs on my phone.'
scoringAnchors.rationale: Band 4 — familiar topic vocabulary, basic adjectives ('very much', 'happy', 'many'), no idiomatic items, no paraphrase under pressure.

scoringAnchors.example: 'I like pop music — well, mostly the type with a strong beat. When I am working, it helps me — I think the word is concentrate? It helps me focus. I have also started listening to older music recently, like rock from the 80s, which is quite different.'
scoringAnchors.rationale: Band 5.5 — successful paraphrase emerging ('the type with a strong beat', 'I think the word is concentrate? It helps me focus' — circumlocution around a known gap, recovered to 'focus'). Vocabulary remains literal across the response; the RUB-LR descriptor reserves figurative and less-common items for the next band up.

scoringAnchors.example: 'I'd say my taste leans towards underground electronic — the kind of stuff that hasn't quite broken into the mainstream. There's something compelling about discovering an artist before they blow up. That said, I've got nothing against a good chart hit when the production is on point.'
scoringAnchors.rationale: Band 7 — varied vocabulary used flexibly ('underground', 'mainstream', 'broken into'), idiomatic items first appear here ('blow up', 'on point'), nuanced concessive ('that said'), precise collocations. Idiom and less-common vocabulary are first required at this band per the RUB-LR descriptor.

promptGuidance.whenHigh: Strong LR. Push topical breadth — abstract Part 3 themes (technology ethics, intergenerational change), demand idiomatic precision, mark unnatural collocations explicitly.
promptGuidance.whenLow: Limited LR. Stay on familiar Part 1 topics (food, weather, weekend), recycle the same vocabulary intentionally, model paraphrase ('another way to say X is Y'), introduce 1-2 new high-utility items per session.
promptGuidance.whenMedium: Mid-band LR. Target circumlocution drills ('describe X without using its name'), flag repetitive vocabulary in feedback, push for one less-common item per long turn.

parameters.promptTemplate:
RUBRIC[skill_lexical_resource_lr] — IELTS Lexical Resource (TRANSCRIPT-ONLY — vendor cannot see vocabulary).
Score the LEARNER on Lexical Resource using IELTS Public Band Descriptors. Output a number 0-1 (IELTS band / 9) OR null if insufficient evidence.

IELTS BAND ANCHORS (HF-active slots):
- Band 3 (0.333): Very basic vocabulary; cannot discuss most topics; meaning often unclear from word choice.
- Band 4 (0.444): Sufficient vocabulary for familiar topics only; rarely paraphrases; frequent inappropriate word choice.
- Band 5.5 (0.611): Successful paraphrase emerging across familiar AND unfamiliar topics; vocabulary remains mostly literal. Limited flexibility; less-common items may appear but are not required to score at this band — do not require or reward figurative or less-common items here.
- Band 7 (0.778): Uses vocabulary flexibly across a variety of topics; some less-common and idiomatic items with occasional inaccuracy; paraphrases effectively when stuck. Idiom and less-common vocabulary FIRST become required at this band — they are the lift from the previous band.

WHAT TO LOOK FOR:
- Range: count distinct content words; note variety of topical lexis, less-common items, idiomatic chunks.
- Precision: are collocations conventional? Is the right word chosen for the meaning?
- Flexibility: when the learner doesn't know a word, do they paraphrase or stop? Is the paraphrase effective?

NULL HANDLING (operator rule — never fabricate):
If the transcript has fewer than ~40 words of learner speech, OR the learner only echoed back the tutor's vocabulary without producing original content, set this score to NULL and hasLearnerEvidence to false. Do NOT default to 0.5. An honest empty band protects the EMA.

EVIDENCE: Cite one to three short transcript quotes (≤15 words each) showing range, precision, or paraphrase attempts.

parameters.name: grammatical_range_and_accuracy
parameters.description: IELTS Grammatical Range & Accuracy — variety of sentence structures, complexity (subordination, conditionals, modals), and error frequency/gravity. TRANSCRIPT-ONLY — vendor audio cannot judge grammar.
parameters.formula: ielts_band(gra) / 9 — normalised against the IELTS Public Band Descriptors for Grammatical Range & Accuracy.

subMetrics.name: Range
subMetrics.description: Variety of structures used — simple, compound, complex; conditionals, passives, modals, subordinate clauses

definitions.high: Uses a wide range of structures flexibly including complex forms (conditionals, relative clauses, modals)
definitions.low: Mostly simple SVO sentences; few or no subordinate or conditional structures

subMetrics.name: Accuracy
subMetrics.description: Frequency, type, and gravity of grammatical errors

definitions.high: Most sentences error-free; errors are minor and do not impede communication (e.g. occasional article slips)
definitions.low: Frequent basic errors (subject-verb agreement, tense, word order) that often impede meaning

subMetrics.name: Complexity
subMetrics.description: Use of complex constructions (subordination, embedding, hedging modals)

definitions.high: Embeds clauses naturally; hedges with modals; uses passives and conditionals when context calls for them
definitions.low: Cannot produce complex constructions even when prompted; sticks to short main clauses

interpretationScale.label: Band 3 — Limited User
interpretationScale.implication: Basic sentence forms with limited accuracy; rarely produces subordinate clauses

interpretationScale.label: Band 4 — Limited User
interpretationScale.implication: Basic sentence forms; some complex structures attempted but with errors causing comprehension problems

interpretationScale.label: Band 5.5 — Modest User
interpretationScale.implication: Uses a mix of simple and complex structures with limited flexibility; some errors but meaning is mostly clear

interpretationScale.label: Band 7 — Good User
interpretationScale.implication: A range of structures flexibly used; error-free sentences are frequent; both simple and complex sentences are used effectively despite some errors. No single named structure is required — flexibility + accuracy across the inventory is what matters.

interpretationScale.label: Band 8+ — Very Good / Expert User
interpretationScale.implication: Wide range of structures used flexibly; the majority of sentences are error-free, with only occasional inappropriacies

scoringAnchors.example: 'I go school every day. Yesterday I go shop. Tomorrow I go park. I like.'
scoringAnchors.rationale: Band 3 — no tense marking, no articles, no subordination; meaning recoverable but errors pervasive.

scoringAnchors.example: 'I am going to the school every day. When I was young, I am going to the park with my brother. I think it is good.'
scoringAnchors.rationale: Band 4 — attempts continuous and past forms but with tense errors; one subordinate clause attempted ('when I was young') with mixed accuracy.

scoringAnchors.example: 'I usually go to work by metro, although sometimes if it's raining I might take a taxi instead. When I was younger I used to cycle, but the traffic has got worse over the years.'
scoringAnchors.rationale: Band 5.5 — mix of simple and complex (conditional 'if', concession 'although', present perfect); minor errors don't impede meaning.

scoringAnchors.example: 'The commute has actually shaped my routine quite a bit. I used to drive in every day, but since the office moved, I take the train — which I'd recommend to anyone who values reading time. There are still occasional delays that I have to work around, though those are rarely a problem.'
scoringAnchors.rationale: Band 7 — flexibility across a range of complex structures: relative clause ('which I'd recommend'), modality ('used to', 'have to', 'would recommend'), present perfect ('has shaped', 'has moved'), passive-style framing ('the office moved'), nominal complement clauses ('anyone who values reading time'). Error-free sentences are frequent. No specific named structure is required at Band 7 per the public IELTS descriptor — flexibility plus accuracy across the inventory is the bar.

promptGuidance.whenHigh: Strong GRA. Push complex hypotheticals ('if you had to choose...'), demand reported speech across tenses, mark over-formal register if it appears.
promptGuidance.whenLow: Limited GRA. Recycle a single tense per topic, drill SVO accuracy explicitly, introduce ONE complex form per session (e.g. 'because' clauses), don't penalise minor errors that don't block meaning.
promptGuidance.whenMedium: Mid-band GRA. Target subordination explicitly ('which', 'although', 'whereas'), prompt for conditional forms in Part 3, give precise error correction on the highest-frequency error type.

parameters.promptTemplate:
RUBRIC[skill_grammatical_range_and_accuracy_gra] — IELTS Grammatical Range & Accuracy (TRANSCRIPT-ONLY — vendor cannot judge grammar).
Score the LEARNER on Grammatical Range & Accuracy using IELTS Public Band Descriptors. Output a number 0-1 (IELTS band / 9) OR null if insufficient evidence.

IELTS BAND ANCHORS (HF-active slots):
- Band 3 (0.333): Basic sentence forms only; limited accuracy; rarely produces subordinate clauses.
- Band 4 (0.444): Basic sentence forms; some complex structures attempted but errors cause comprehension problems.
- Band 5.5 (0.611): Mix of simple AND complex with limited flexibility; errors present but meaning mostly clear.
- Band 7 (0.778): A range of structures flexibly used — subordinate clauses, modality (would/could/might/should), passive voice, complex tense, relative clauses — no specific named structure is required per the public IELTS descriptor. Error-free sentences are frequent. Both simple and complex sentences are used effectively despite some errors. A few basic errors may persist.

WHAT TO LOOK FOR:
- Range: count distinct structure types (SVO, SVOO, passive, conditional, modal-hedged, relative clauses, reported speech).
- Accuracy: count basic errors (subject-verb agreement, tense, word order, article use) — note frequency AND whether they impede meaning.
- Complexity: are complex constructions attempted naturally or only when forced? Hedging modals ('might', 'would', 'could')?

NULL HANDLING (operator rule — never fabricate):
If the transcript has fewer than ~40 words of learner speech, OR the learner only gave one-word answers throughout, set this score to NULL and hasLearnerEvidence to false. Do NOT default to a 'balanced' 0.5. An honest empty band protects the EMA from corruption.

EVIDENCE: Cite one to three short transcript quotes (≤15 words each) demonstrating range, accuracy, or error patterns.

parameters.name: pronunciation
parameters.description: IELTS Pronunciation — dual-confidence scoring. Scored from transcript cues (self-corrections, tutor pronunciation feedback, repair patterns, hesitations, length variance, long-turn cleanliness) whenever there is any speech evidence; confidence is capped at 'medium' from transcript alone. When prosody vendor is wired (epic #2135 S3), the prosody-raw consumer augments this slot and raises confidence to 'high'.
parameters.formula: ielts_band(p) / 9 — normalised against the IELTS Public Band Descriptors for Pronunciation. NULL when transcript cues are absent.

subMetrics.name: Intelligibility Cues
subMetrics.description: Transcript evidence of intelligibility — tutor confirmation requests, repeat-back signals, mishearings

definitions.high: No tutor 'sorry, can you repeat?' or 'did you mean X?' moments; tutor flows naturally through learner turns
definitions.low: Frequent tutor requests for repetition, explicit confirmations ('did you say X?'), or transcript-noted mishearings

subMetrics.name: Self-correction Cues
subMetrics.description: Transcript evidence of pronunciation-related self-correction or pronunciation-targeted tutor feedback

definitions.high: No pronunciation self-correction needed; tutor offers no pronunciation feedback
definitions.low: Frequent self-correction targeting a sound or word ('I mean — wuh — work'); tutor provides explicit pronunciation feedback ('try saying it like THIS')

subMetrics.name: Register / Stress Cues
subMetrics.description: Transcript-visible cues to sentence stress, intonation, register (e.g. ALL-CAPS transcribed emphasis, exclamation-marked utterances, transcript-noted flat delivery)

definitions.high: Transcript shows natural intonation-marked utterances where context supports them; appropriate register shifts
definitions.low: Flat or monotonous delivery noted in transcript; register-inappropriate utterances (e.g. casual register in formal Part 2)

interpretationScale.label: Band 3 — Limited User
interpretationScale.implication: Pronunciation requires frequent listener effort; some/much speech may be unintelligible

interpretationScale.label: Band 4 — Limited User
interpretationScale.implication: Uses a limited range of pronunciation features with mixed control; mispronunciations cause comprehension difficulty at times

interpretationScale.label: Band 5.5 — Modest User
interpretationScale.implication: Some effective use of pronunciation features but with limited control; mispronunciations occur but do not generally reduce intelligibility

interpretationScale.label: Band 7 — Good User
interpretationScale.implication: Shows all positive features of band 6 (some effective use of features, sustained pronunciation control) plus some of the features of band 8 (wide range, easy to understand)

interpretationScale.label: Band 8+ — Very Good / Expert User
interpretationScale.implication: Uses a wide range of pronunciation features with precision and subtlety; sustained flexible use of features; easy to understand throughout

scoringAnchors.example: Tutor (repeated 3 times in transcript): 'Sorry, could you say that again?' / Learner: 'I sed... I sed... I am... [unintelligible].' / Tutor: 'Did you say SAID? Let me know if I'm hearing right.'
scoringAnchors.rationale: Band 3 — frequent unintelligibility, multiple repetition requests, tutor cannot reliably parse the utterance.

scoringAnchors.example: Learner: 'I work in a — uh — bank. The bank. I wuh-wuh-work in the bank.' Tutor: 'Got it — and what do you do at the bank?'
scoringAnchors.rationale: Band 4 — visible self-correction on 'work', mild intelligibility issue surmounted, tutor able to continue but with some friction.

scoringAnchors.example: Learner: 'I'm an analyst at — at an investment bank. Most of my day is spent on financial modelling.' Tutor: 'Right — investment banking. And how long have you done that?' (No mishearings, one minor restart noted.)
scoringAnchors.rationale: Band 5.5 — mostly intelligible, one minor false-start, no repetition requests from tutor.

scoringAnchors.example: Learner full Part 2 long turn delivered without tutor interruption, transcript shows no 'sorry?' / 'pardon?' / 'did you say...' from tutor across 90 seconds of speech. One natural intonation cue noted ('REALLY tricky, that one').
scoringAnchors.rationale: Band 7 — sustained intelligibility across an extended turn, transcript-visible stress cue used appropriately, no friction in tutor uptake.

promptGuidance.whenHigh: Strong P from transcript signal. Push connected speech features in Part 3 (long abstract turns), avoid over-correction. When prosody vendor is wired, the augmentation chip will confirm.
promptGuidance.whenLow: Transcript shows intelligibility friction. Slow tutor pace, confirm-back what was heard, target the highest-frequency problem sound explicitly. Mark P as null if friction is structural rather than learner-pronunciation (e.g. ASR limitations).
promptGuidance.whenMedium: Mid-band P from transcript. Do one targeted minimal-pair drill per session if the same sound reoccurs in self-corrections. Otherwise focus on FC/LR/GRA — pronunciation evidence is sparse in pure transcript and a vendor augmentation is the better long-term signal.

parameters.promptTemplate:
RUBRIC[skill_pronunciation_p] — IELTS Pronunciation (DUAL-CONFIDENCE SCORING — transcript cues today, vendor-augmented later via epic #2135 S3).
Score the LEARNER on Pronunciation using IELTS Public Band Descriptors. Output a number 0-1 (IELTS band / 9) WHENEVER there is any speech evidence in the transcript, PLUS a confidence tier ('low' | 'medium' | 'high'). Return null ONLY if the transcript contains no learner speech at all.

DUAL-CONFIDENCE DESIGN (read this first):
P is scored differently from LR / GRA / FC. Pronunciation evidence is inherently weaker in pure-transcript mode than it will be when a vendor signal lands. To stay honest WITHOUT zeroing every call's P slot, score P from transcript cues whenever there is any speech evidence (fillers, hesitations, repair patterns, repetition, length variance, self-correction, tutor friction) and STAMP the confidence accordingly:
- Transcript-only evidence → confidence ≤ medium. Never 'high' from transcript alone.
- The S3 prosody-augmentation writer (different parameter, post-S3) raises confidence to 'high' when vendor signal arrives.
This design means P is ALWAYS scored when there is any speech, never null-when-vendor-absent, but confidence is honest. The downstream EMA aggregator (SKILL-AGG-001) is confidence-aware once #2135 S3 lands.

CONFIDENCE TIERS (transcript-only):
- 'low': fewer than ~3 distinct cues across the call; the band judgment is a best-effort read of a sparse signal.
- 'medium': multiple distinct cues (tutor confirmations + learner self-corrections + a clean long turn, or similar combinations) — enough to defend the band from transcript alone.
- 'high': RESERVED for vendor-augmented runs. Do NOT emit 'high' from transcript-only signal.

IELTS BAND ANCHORS (HF-active slots):
- Band 3 (0.333): Frequent unintelligibility, multiple tutor repetition requests, parts of utterances cannot be parsed.
- Band 4 (0.444): Limited range of pronunciation features with mixed control; mispronunciations sometimes cause comprehension difficulty.
- Band 5.5 (0.611): Some effective use of pronunciation features with limited control; mispronunciations occur but generally don't reduce intelligibility.
- Band 7 (0.778): Sustained pronunciation control across extended turns; range of features (stress, intonation) used appropriately; easy to understand throughout.

WHAT TO LOOK FOR (TRANSCRIPT CUES — any speech evidence counts):
- Tutor signals: 'sorry?', 'pardon?', 'did you say X?', 'could you repeat that?' — count frequency.
- Learner self-corrections targeting a sound or word ('wuh-wuh-work', 'I sed — I mean SAID').
- Repair patterns: filler density ('um', 'uh'), false-starts, mid-word restarts, length variance across turns.
- Explicit tutor pronunciation feedback ('try saying it like THIS', 'the stress goes on the second syllable').
- Transcript-visible stress/intonation cues (ALL-CAPS emphasis, exclamation marks, transcript-noted flat delivery).
- Long-turn cleanliness: a sustained Part 2 turn delivered without tutor interruption is positive evidence (Band 6+ territory).

NULL HANDLING (operator rule — never fabricate):
Return null + hasLearnerEvidence: false ONLY when the transcript contains no learner speech at all (or fewer than ~40 words, the same threshold the other criteria use). When there IS speech but the pronunciation cues are sparse, score honestly at confidence: 'low' — do NOT default to null and do NOT default to a 'safe midpoint'. Sparse cues + honest low confidence is the right answer; absent speech is the only null case.

EVIDENCE: Cite one to three transcript quotes showing the cues you found (or the long-turn cleanliness signal). State the confidence tier explicitly in your response.

responseShape.description: The LLM MUST return a strict JSON object matching this shape. Per-criterion null scores are mandatory when evidence is insufficient. The `pronunciation` slot additionally carries a `confidence` tier — dual-confidence scoring per CON-IELTS-002.

pronunciation.value: number 0-1 (IELTS band / 9) OR null (null ONLY when no learner speech at all)
pronunciation.confidence: string enum 'low' | 'medium' | 'high' — transcript-only evidence MUST cap at 'medium'; 'high' is reserved for the S3 prosody-augmentation pathway. Omit only when value is null.

evidence.fluency_and_coherence: string — 1-3 short transcript quotes justifying the FC band, OR 'insufficient evidence' if null
evidence.lexical_resource: string — 1-3 short transcript quotes justifying the LR band, OR 'insufficient evidence' if null
evidence.grammatical_range_and_accuracy: string — 1-3 short transcript quotes justifying the GRA band, OR 'insufficient evidence' if null
evidence.pronunciation: string — 1-3 transcript pronunciation cues with confidence tier stated, OR 'no learner speech to score' if null

schema.hasLearnerEvidence: boolean — false when ALL four scores are null (transcript had <40 words of learner speech); true otherwise

related.title: Skill EMA aggregator — consumes CallScore rows on skill_* parameter ids
Skill measurement contract — declares the 4 IELTS skill parameter ids (FC/LR/GRA/P)
IELTS scoring as canonical MEASURE specs — parent epic
